methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Late Stage → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.2x) to $5.7B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Transportation (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 1 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 2 same-sector peers exist in Transportation, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
The Boring Company is a late-stage Transportation company founded by Elon Musk with a current post-money valuation of $5.7B, having raised $675M to date. The peer universe in Transportation is sparse: two private comparables (Rivian and Einride) exist, though neither qualifies as a tight same-stage or adjacent-stage match, limiting reliance on peer multiples.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable approach. Using the last post-money valuation of $5.7B as the base and applying a 1.2x multiplier typical of late-stage advancement yields $6.8B. This method directly reflects risk-adjusted progression from the company's confirmed financing history rather than external benchmarks.

The peer-median approach was not applied due to insufficient narrow peer density. With zero same-stage and zero adjacent-stage comparables in Transportation, a peer-based valuation would lack statistical rigor and risk introducing distortion from misaligned risk profiles.

The triangulated range spans $5.8B (floor anchored to recent post-money) to $7.9B, with a median of $6.8B supported by stage progression. Key uncertainties include the depth of Transportation sector comparable data and the company's path to next-stage funding. The single-method foundation of this analysis reflects data constraints rather than conviction; widening the comp universe or obtaining additional financing signals would materially strengthen the valuation.
